rationale: A0 shows netsh.exe running at High integrity under a standard user account (host-01\user-01) to add an inbound TCP firewall rule on port 450 with the name '[x] testing', spawned from a bare cmd.exe with no visible parent command context. EV-001 confirms the rule was written to the Windows Firewall registry key, and EV-003/EV-004 confirm the rule was deleted ~5 seconds later — the transient add-then-delete pattern is consistent with a probe or cleanup step (e.g., confirming firewall manipulation capability or removing evidence of a backdoor rule), but could also reflect a legitimate test script. The '[x] testing' name and non-standard port 450 are weak indicators of malicious intent; however, the elevated integrity level for a standard user, the ephemeral nature of the rule, and the anonymous cmd.exe parent warrant further investigation to determine how elevated privileges were obtained and what initiated the cmd.exe session.
